title: podman 1.9.0 rootless on CentOS error: cgroup namespaces aren't enabled in the kernel: OCI runtime error
body:
**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

Fresh CentOS 7.7 kvm-guest w/ Kubic CentOS7 repo added, installs podman 1.9.0 however no containers can be started:

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. fresh centos7 installed

2. setup kubic centos7 libcontainers-stable repo

3. launch nginx container

**Describe the results you received:**

```
[test@test ~]$ podman run -d -p 8080:80 --name nginx docker.io/library/nginx:alpine
WARN[0000] The cgroupv2 manager is set to systemd but there is no systemd user session available 
WARN[0000] For using systemd, you may need to login using an user session 
WARN[0000] Alternatively, you can enable lingering with: `loginctl enable-linger 1000` (possibly as root) 
WARN[0000] Falling back to --cgroup-manager=cgroupfs    
Trying to pull docker.io/library/nginx:alpine...
Getting image source signatures
Copying blob b14da7a62044 done  
Copying blob aad63a933944 done  
Copying config 29b49a39bc done  
Writing manifest to image destination
Storing signatures
Error: cgroup namespaces aren't enabled in the kernel: OCI runtime error
```

**Describe the results you expected:**

container launching normally

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
[test@test ~]$ podman version
WARN[0000] The cgroupv2 manager is set to systemd but there is no systemd user session available 
WARN[0000] For using systemd, you may need to login using an user session 
WARN[0000] Alternatively, you can enable lingering with: `loginctl enable-linger 1000` (possibly as root) 
WARN[0000] Falling back to --cgroup-manager=cgroupfs    
Version:            1.9.0
RemoteAPI Version:  1
Go Version:         go1.13.6
OS/Arch:            linux/amd64
```

**Output of `podman info --debug`:**

```
[test@test ~]$ podman info --debug
WARN[0000] The cgroupv2 manager is set to systemd but there is no systemd user session available 
WARN[0000] For using systemd, you may need to login using an user session 
WARN[0000] Alternatively, you can enable lingering with: `loginctl enable-linger 1000` (possibly as root) 
WARN[0000] Falling back to --cgroup-manager=cgroupfs

      commit: 67b92f062188d9cb6472b428855432c9f35efcf5

  slirp4netns:

      libslirp: 4.2.0
  swapFree: 0
  swapTotal: 0
  uptime: 6m 15.53s
registries:
  search:
  - registry.fedoraproject.org
  - registry.access.redhat.com
  - registry.centos.org
  - docker.io
store:
  configFile: /home/test/.config/containers/storage.conf
  containerStore:
    number: 1
    paused: 0
    running: 0
    stopped: 1
  graphDriverName: vfs
  graphOptions: {}
  graphRoot: /home/test/.local/share/containers/storage
  graphStatus: {}
  imageStore:
    number: 1
  runRoot: /run/user/1000/containers
  volumePath: /home/test/.local/share/containers/storage/volumes
```

**Package info (e.g. output of `rpm -q podman` or `apt list podman`):**

```
[test@test ~]$ rpm -qi podman
Name        : podman
Epoch       : 0
Version     : 1.9.0
Release     : 1.1.el7
Architecture: x86_64
Install Date: Tue Apr 21 23:24:12 2020
Group       : Unspecified
Size        : 78805972

Source RPM  : podman-1.9.0-1.1.el7.src.rpm
Build Date  : Thu Apr 16 15:54:18 2020
Build Host  : lamb28
```

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**

<details><summary>From a fresh CentOS 7 host - here's the full session of commands for your review, from adding the repo to installing the packages fresh, to logging in as a non-root user and attempting to run a container</summary>

comments.body:
some googling around and it appears to be coming [from this runc go-file](https://github.com/opencontainers/runc/blob/master/libcontainer/configs/validate/validator.go#L125) 

<details><summary>including my runc version</summary>

```
[test@test ~]$ rpm -qi runc
Name        : runc
Epoch       : 2
Version     : 1.0.0
Release     : 15.1.el7
Architecture: x86_64
Install Date: Tue Apr 21 23:24:07 2020
Group       : Unspecified
Size        : 12982071
License     : ASL 2.0
Signature   : RSA/SHA256, Wed Feb 19 20:51:42 2020, Key ID 4d64390375060aa4
Source RPM  : runc-1.0.0-15.1.el7.src.rpm
Build Date  : Wed Feb 19 20:51:37 2020
[...]
```

</details>

and confirming i have these under `/proc/self/ns` (note no `cgroup` entry):

```
[test@test ~]$ ls -l /proc/self/ns/  
total 0
lrwxrwxrwx 1 test users 0 Apr 22 00:05 ipc -> ipc:[4026531839]
lrwxrwxrwx 1 test users 0 Apr 22 00:05 mnt -> mnt:[4026531840]
lrwxrwxrwx 1 test users 0 Apr 22 00:05 net -> net:[4026531956]
lrwxrwxrwx 1 test users 0 Apr 22 00:05 pid -> pid:[4026531836]
lrwxrwxrwx 1 test users 0 Apr 22 00:05 user -> user:[4026531837]
lrwxrwxrwx 1 test users 0 Apr 22 00:05 uts -> uts:[4026531838]
```

what needs to be done to make podman work again with these new changes where it now defaults to expecting that entry in `/proc/self/ns` ?  how exactly does one go about enabling these cgroup namespaces in the kernel? i don't see any obvious sysctl's (and being it's CentOS, it's not practical to recompile the kernel..

If we are making podman 1.9.0 available for centos 7 machines, then we need to make sure we ship a containers.conf file in /usr/share/containers/containers.conf with the correct configuration for this system.

https://github.com/containers/common/blob/master/pkg/config/containers.conf

If you copy this file to /etc/containers/containers.conf and modify the settings, you should get it working properly on Centos7

in the CentOS 7 rpm the only .conf file included in `/usr/share/containers` is called `libpod.conf`:

```
[root@test ~]# rpm -ql podman | grep usr/share/containers
/usr/share/containers/libpod.conf
[root@test ~]# ls -l $(!!)
ls -l $(rpm -ql podman | grep usr/share/containers)
-rw-r--r-- 1 root root 5993 Apr 15 14:51 /usr/share/containers/libpod.conf
```

Placing the above linked containers.conf at that location does not resolve the issue:

```
[root@test ~]# curl -sLo /usr/share/containers/containers.conf 'https://raw.githubusercontent.com/containers/common/master/pkg/config/containers.conf'
[root@test ~]# ls -l !!:2
ls -l /usr/share/containers/containers.conf
-rw-r--r-- 1 root root 13044 Apr 22 11:36 /usr/share/containers/containers.conf
```

(and logging back in to test rootless):

```
$ ssh test@192.168.0.98
test@192.168.0.98's password: 
Last login: Wed Apr 22 00:10:20 2020 from 192.168.0.52
[test@test ~]$ podman rm nginx
a08bf18facd6bd50ccc1fbbb63ea86c5768496ef10511e5a35a0a511a6de8a9b
[test@test ~]$ podman run -d -p 8080:80 --name nginx docker.io/library/nginx:alpine
Error: cgroup namespaces aren't enabled in the kernel: OCI runtime error
```

Ahh, @rhatdan you _did_ also say "..and update the settings", i missed that part ~(but don't know what settings you recommend are updated).  Happy to do testing to confirm getting the RPM working if you let me what setting need to be set to what please.~ I had a look and it seems changing [this line](https://github.com/containers/common/blob/a76ab0b7211cb13caa725153b56878147fa93293/pkg/config/containers.conf#L48) to "host" makes podman run.. is this the setting you are recommending be set for CentOS 7 (also curious what this recommendation is based on?)

i'm not clear which package that file needs to belong to, containers-common? @lsm5 is this something you'd be fixing?

---

Also curiously, while examining that containers.conf, i notice [some unicode quotation marks](https://github.com/containers/common/blob/master/pkg/config/containers.conf#L95) slipped in:

```
[root@test ~]# grep --color='auto' -P -n "[\x80-\xFF]" /usr/share/containers/containers.conf 
95:#  ?nofile?=?1280:2560?,
108:# container. The special value ?none? can be specified to disable creation of
128:# ?/usr/share/containers/oci/hooks.d?,
223:# UIDs are allocated from the ?container? UIDs listed in
244:# Valid options ?systemd? or ?cgroupfs?
```

Thankfully they are commented, but if someone were to uncomment one of them, these "smart quote marks" like to cause all sorts of time-wasting havoc :-) been there, lost hours trying to figure why things broke.. figure i'd point these out, as per [git blame](https://github.com/containers/common/blame/master/pkg/config/containers.conf#L95), it looks like it was [this commit](https://github.com/containers/common/commit/bd0a08c617ccba78b3d72b354d4680251e73b1e5) that introduced these @rhatdan
Does changing cgroups-manager to cgroupfs help to fix this?  Can  you set this cgroupns to host, but I am not sure this will fix the problem

for me, changing only `cgroups-manager` to `cgroupfs` (from the default of `systemd`) does not fix _this_ issue (about the complaint of cgroup namespaces not being enabled in the kernel, and not launching rootless containers), no.. 

```
[test@test ~]$ cat /etc/containers/containers.conf
[engine]
cgroup_manager = "systemd"
[test@test ~]$ podman run -d -p 8080:80 --name nginx docker.io/library/nginx:alpine
WARN[0000] The cgroupv2 manager is set to systemd but there is no systemd user session available 
WARN[0000] For using systemd, you may need to login using an user session 
WARN[0000] Alternatively, you can enable lingering with: `loginctl enable-linger 1000` (possibly as root) 
WARN[0000] Falling back to --cgroup-manager=cgroupfs    
Error: cgroup namespaces aren't enabled in the kernel: OCI runtime error
[test@test ~]$ sudo sed -i -e 's/cgroup_manager = "systemd"/cgroup_manager = "cgroupfs"/' /etc/containers/containers.conf 
[test@test ~]$ cat /etc/containers/containers.conf
[engine]
cgroup_manager = "cgroupfs"
[test@test ~]$ podman rm nginx
07fec9e4495139b59529b18466460e06738f3de7e1cb174d97d7eedd24ca502f
[test@test ~]$ podman run -d -p 8080:80 --name nginx docker.io/library/nginx:alpine
Error: cgroup namespaces aren't enabled in the kernel: OCI runtime error
```

but as a side-effect, it does fix [this noisy-warnings issue](https://github.com/containers/libpod/issues/5906#issuecomment-617319390) instead

i need to actually have `cgroupns` set to `host` (instead of the default value of `private`), regardless of cgroup_manager being already set to cgroupfs, in order to be able to launch containers in rootless mode on CentOS 7:

```
[test@test ~]$ cat /etc/containers/containers.conf
[containers]
cgroupns = "private"

[engine]
cgroup_manager = "cgroupfs"
[test@test ~]$ podman run -d -p 8080:80 --name nginx docker.io/library/nginx:alpine
Error: cgroup namespaces aren't enabled in the kernel: OCI runtime error
[test@test ~]$ podman rm nginx 2>/dev/null
72adc8bccc662a5ee6f9cbed5c708f6c07037bdc69746110d57a05ad132abebc
[test@test ~]$ sudo sed -i -e 's/cgroupns = "private"/cgroupns = "host"/' /etc/containers/containers.conf
[test@test ~]$ cat /etc/containers/containers.conf
[containers]
cgroupns = "host"

[engine]
cgroup_manager = "cgroupfs"
[test@test ~]$ podman run -d -p 8080:80 --name nginx docker.io/library/nginx:alpine
91aa8ae8f6e91e84f117c2e7817683538be516a96df8a73ab6d91fcd27b1583f
[test@test ~]$ podman ps -a
CONTAINER ID  IMAGE                           COMMAND               CREATED        STATUS            PORTS                 NAMES
91aa8ae8f6e9  docker.io/library/nginx:alpine  nginx -g daemon o...  4 seconds ago  Up 3 seconds ago  0.0.0.0:8080->80/tcp  nginx
```
@lsm5 We are going to need to ship a specially configured containers.conf for centos7, to turn off cgroupns

> @lsm5 We are going to need to ship a specially configured containers.conf for centos7, to turn off cgroupns

Do I just comment it out, or does it need to  be `cgroupns = "host"`

From my experience, what's commented is usually the default, and the default does not work on CentOS 7.. i think it needs to explicitly set to "host" to work.  

Are you able to repro it w/ and w/o that setting to see if it makes a difference for you?  It might be better if i'm not the only data point (and i'm pretty certain i'm not the only one still using CentOS 7 :-) )

the fix solves the issue but I don't think it is the correct fix.

We shouldn't change the default for cgroupns.  On cgroup v1 it should still default to host otherwise it is a breaking change.

I think we need to handle it from Podman.  If the user doesn't override it in the conf file, we need to default to `host` on cgroup v1 and `private` on cgroup v2.

> the fix solves the issue but I don't think it is the correct fix.
> 
> We shouldn't change the default for cgroupns. On cgroup v1 it should still default to host otherwise it is a breaking change.
> 
> I think we need to handle it from Podman. If the user doesn't override it in the conf file, we need to default to `host` on cgroup v1 and `private` on cgroup v2.

I can have different settings for v1 and v2 in the rpm build process.

> I can have different settings for v1 and v2 in the rpm build process.

that cannot be a build time configuration.  Users can switch between v1 and v2

On Centos 7, there is no support for cgroupns.  So we should default for root and rootless to use host.
On RHEL8/Centos 8. We can use the patch that you got into common.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

Believe this one is fixed in containers/common, should be in Podman
v1.9.2 and up.
